title: ROS Fundamentals
objective: Understand the architecture of ROS 2, its communication model, key tools, and how to structure a basic robotics project using the Robot Operating System framework.
beginnerExplanation:
Building a robot from scratch means writing software for sensors, motors, path planning, obstacle avoidance, visualisation, and more. If every roboticist had to write all of this from the ground up, progress would be painfully slow. The Robot Operating System (ROS) solves this by providing a shared framework — a common set of tools, libraries, and conventions that let roboticists reuse code and build on each other's work.

Despite its name, ROS is not an operating system like Windows or Linux. It runs on top of Linux (primarily Ubuntu) and provides a middleware layer — a communication system that lets different parts of your robot software talk to each other. Each functional piece of software is called a node. A camera driver node publishes images. A perception node subscribes to those images and publishes detected objects. A planning node subscribes to detected objects and publishes movement commands. A motor driver node subscribes to movement commands and drives the motors.

This publisher-subscriber model is the heart of ROS. Nodes communicate by publishing messages to named topics. Any node can subscribe to any topic. This creates a modular, loosely-coupled architecture where you can swap out components easily — replace one camera driver with another, or one planning algorithm with a better one, without rewriting the rest of the system.

ROS 2 (the current generation, released from 2018 onwards) is built on DDS (Data Distribution Service), an industrial-grade communication middleware. Compared to ROS 1, ROS 2 supports real-time operation, has better security features, works on multiple platforms (not just Linux), and supports multi-robot systems more naturally. Most new robotics projects should use ROS 2.
deeperExplanation:
ROS 2 architecture is built around several core concepts. Nodes are the basic computational units — each is a process that performs a specific function. Topics are named channels for streaming data between nodes using a publish-subscribe pattern. Services provide a request-response pattern for synchronous operations (e.g. 'take a picture and return it'). Actions extend services for long-running tasks that provide feedback (e.g. 'navigate to waypoint and report progress').

The tf2 (transform) library is one of ROS's most powerful features. In a robot, there are many coordinate frames — the base frame, each sensor frame, the end effector frame, the map frame. tf2 maintains a tree of transformations between all these frames and allows any node to query the transform between any two frames at any time. This seemingly simple capability is crucial: when your LiDAR detects an obstacle, tf2 tells you where that obstacle is relative to the robot base, or relative to the map, automatically accounting for sensor mounting position and robot motion.

ROS 2 packages are the unit of software distribution. Each package contains nodes, message definitions, launch files, configuration files, and documentation. The build system (colcon) compiles packages, resolving dependencies automatically. Launch files (written in Python or XML) describe how to start multiple nodes with specific parameters, enabling complex robot configurations to be launched with a single command.

Gazebo is the standard simulation environment for ROS. It provides physics simulation (rigid body dynamics, collisions, gravity), sensor simulation (camera, LiDAR, IMU with realistic noise models), and actuator simulation. You can develop and test your entire robot software stack in Gazebo before deploying to real hardware — a practice that is essential for safety-critical applications like autonomous vehicles.

The ROS ecosystem includes thousands of open-source packages. Navigation2 (Nav2) provides a complete autonomous navigation stack. MoveIt 2 provides motion planning for robot arms. The perception pipeline includes packages for point cloud processing, object detection, and SLAM. CSIRO's Data61 in Australia has contributed significant packages to the ROS ecosystem, particularly for field robotics applications.

For Australian robotics companies and researchers, ROS 2 is becoming the standard. Emesent's Hovermap autonomous drone mapping system, CSIRO's autonomous underground vehicles, and numerous university research projects are built on ROS. The move to ROS 2 brings real-time capabilities that are essential for safety-critical mining and agricultural applications.
keyTakeaways: ROS is middleware, not an operating system — it provides communication infrastructure and libraries for robotics
Nodes communicate via topics (pub-sub), services (request-response), and actions (long-running tasks with feedback)
tf2 manages coordinate frame transformations — essential for multi-sensor robots
ROS 2 adds real-time support, security, and multi-robot capabilities over ROS 1
Gazebo simulation lets you develop and test robot software before deploying to hardware
The ROS ecosystem includes thousands of reusable packages for navigation, manipulation, perception, and more
example: An Australian university robotics team is building an autonomous delivery robot for campus. Using ROS 2, they create separate nodes for: a Velodyne LiDAR driver (publishes point clouds to /lidar/points), a camera driver (publishes images to /camera/image), an IMU driver (publishes to /imu/data), a SLAM node using RTAB-Map (subscribes to LiDAR and camera, publishes to /map and /tf), a Nav2 navigation stack (subscribes to /map and /goal_pose, publishes to /cmd_vel), and a motor controller node (subscribes to /cmd_vel, drives the wheel motors). They test the entire system in Gazebo simulation first, navigating a virtual campus model. When they deploy to the real robot, they only need to swap the simulated sensor drivers for real hardware drivers — the rest of the software stack runs unchanged. This is the power of ROS's modular architecture.
commonMistakes: Starting a new project with ROS 1 instead of ROS 2 — ROS 1 reaches end of life in 2025 and lacks key features
Building monolithic nodes that do everything — ROS works best with small, focused, reusable nodes
Ignoring tf2 and trying to manage coordinate transforms manually — this is error-prone and unnecessary
Not using simulation (Gazebo) before deploying to hardware — debugging on real hardware is slow, expensive, and potentially dangerous
Hardcoding parameters instead of using ROS 2's parameter system — this makes your code inflexible and hard to reconfigure
exercise: Install ROS 2 Humble on Ubuntu 22.04 (follow the official installation guide at docs.ros.org). Complete the beginner tutorials: create a workspace, build a simple publisher node that publishes 'Hello, Robotics!' to a topic, and a subscriber node that prints the messages. Then launch the turtlesim simulator and write a node that commands the turtle to draw a square by publishing geometry_msgs/msg/Twist messages. This exercise introduces you to the fundamental ROS 2 workflow: create nodes, publish and subscribe to topics, and interact with simulated robots.

quiz.question: What is the primary communication pattern used between ROS 2 nodes for streaming data?
quiz.explanation: ROS 2 nodes communicate streaming data through the publish-subscribe pattern via named topics. A node publishes messages to a topic, and any other node can subscribe to that topic to receive the messages. This creates a flexible, decoupled architecture where nodes do not need to know about each other directly.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of tf2 in ROS?
options: It provides a graphical user interface for robot control
It manages coordinate frame transformations between different parts of the robot and its environment
It compresses sensor data for efficient network transmission
It handles user authentication and security
quiz.explanation: tf2 maintains a tree of transformations between all coordinate frames in a robot system — base frame, sensor frames, end effector frame, map frame, and more. Any node can query tf2 to find the transform between any two frames at any point in time, which is essential for tasks like converting LiDAR detections into map coordinates or commanding a robot arm in the base frame.

perspectives.softwareEngineer: As a robotics software engineer, ROS 2 is your daily development framework. You design node architectures, define message interfaces, write launch files, and debug distributed systems using tools like rqt_graph and ros2 topic echo.
perspectives.researcher: As a robotics researcher, ROS lets you build on thousands of existing packages and share your own work with the community. You focus on your novel contribution while leveraging proven implementations for everything else.
perspectives.teamLead: As a robotics team lead, ROS provides a common language and architecture for your team. Clear node boundaries make it easy to divide work, and the standardised interfaces reduce integration risk.
